2026-06-09T08:41:46.9126301Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9126731Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9127250Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9127765Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9128303Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9128726Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9129174Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9129782Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9130338Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9130773Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9131138Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9131553Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9131931Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9132407Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9133515Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9134093Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9134547Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9135020Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9135414Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9135625Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9135957Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9136362Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9136839Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9137328Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m
2026-06-09T08:41:46.9137815Z [36;1m  fi[0m

2026-06-09T08:41:59.3100177Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m

2026-06-09T08:48:25.5420103Z     "growth_strategy_note": "2025-06 | PR #1668 symlink parity fix -- three narrative angles for release and social:\n\n1. SILENT DATA LOSS CLOSED (trust-restoration story). Package authors using symlinks for shared content inside local packages were silently losing files on every `apm install <local-path>`. No error, no warning, just missing content. This is a textbook trust-eroding bug -- the kind that makes people doubt the tool rather than suspect symlinks. Fixing it and saying so plainly ('your shared files were being silently dropped; they are not anymore') is a credibility move. Lead release notes with the user symptom, not the implementation detail.\n\n2. LOCAL/REMOTE INSTALL PARITY ('predictable everywhere'). The README positioning is 'Portable by manifest. Secure by default. Governed by policy.' Parity between local and remote install is a direct line to 'portable.' Remote install materialized symlinks because git checkout does; local install dropped them. This was a fidelity gap in the core value proposition. The fix closes that gap and makes the 'same install, any source' story true. Reusable copy for social: 'local packages and remote packages now behave identically during install -- what you build locally is exactly what your team installs.'\n\n3. ENTERPRISE THREAT MODEL DOCUMENTED (security review accelerator). The security.md update adds a six-property containment guarantee with explicit hard-fail semantics for symlink escape attempts. Enterprise security teams doing APM procurement reviews look for exactly this: threat actor named, boundary defined, fail-closed confirmed. This is not just a correctness fix -- it is a new enterprise evaluation asset. Flag it in the changelog and any enterprise-facing release announcement as 'APM now documents local-install containment guarantees' with a link to the security.md section.\n\nBREAKING CHANGE RISK: escaping-symlink hard-fail is a behavior change. Users who had out-of-root symlinks (even accidentally) that were silently ignored before will now see PathTraversalError on upgrade. This needs an upgrade notice in the CHANGELOG and a one-liner in the release notes: 'audit local packages with `find <pkg> -type l` before upgrading.'\n\nCONTRIBUTOR ACQUISITION: the test file (test_local_content_symlink_deref.py) establishes a clean pattern: NullCommandLogger + tmp_path + _try_symlink + typed coverage gates. This is a good template to highlight in CONTRIBUTING.md as the canonical TDD pattern for bug-fix PRs -- regression trap + security trap + baseline + edge cases, all in one file."

2026-06-09T08:51:23.3037251Z   "persona": "supply-chain-security-expert",

2026-06-09T08:51:24.5475361Z   "summary": "Core POSIX path-traversal fix is sound; three gaps remain: NTFS junction bypass on Windows, RecursionError leaks partial install without cleanup, and a narrow TOCTOU window between validate and copy2.",
2026-06-09T08:51:24.5478868Z   "findings": [
2026-06-09T08:51:24.5479288Z     {
2026-06-09T08:51:24.5485651Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-09T08:51:24.5526228Z       "summary": "NTFS junction points bypass is_symlink check on Windows; traverse without containment guard",
2026-06-09T08:51:28.1639747Z       "rationale": "Python's Path.is_symlink() returns False for NTFS junction points (IO_REPARSE_TAG_MOUNT_POINT reparse tag). On Windows a malicious local package could include a junction pointing to C:\\Windows\\System32. The code's is_symlink() guard returns False, so the entry falls into the elif entry.is_dir() branch and is recursed into via _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated(entry, dst_entry, pkg_root, _visited) with no containment check -- the pkg_root boundary is never applied. The staged copy would contain content from outside the package. This is specific to Windows; Linux and macOS are unaffected because POSIX has no junction concept.",
2026-06-09T08:51:29.3523375Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/install/phases/local_content.py",

Alternatively, document in security.md that local-install symlink containment does not extend to NTFS junctions and note it as a known limitation for Windows.",

2026-06-09T08:51:36.1024083Z     {
2026-06-09T08:51:36.1036318Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-09T08:51:36.1053780Z       "summary": "RecursionError on deep package trees bypasses PathTraversalError cleanup gate, leaving partial install_path",
2026-06-09T08:51:38.7109055Z       "rationale": "_copy_tree_dereferencing_validated is unboundedly recursive with no depth counter. Python's default recursion limit is 1000. Accounting for the surrounding call stack (install pipeline, LocalDependencySource.acquire, _copy_local_package), the effective max directory depth before RecursionError is approximately 200-240 levels. A crafted local package with 250 nested directories triggers RecursionError. The try/except in _copy_local_package catches only PathTraversalError; RecursionError propagates unhandled, skipping the safe_rmtree(install_path, ...) cleanup block. The partial install_path is left on disk. This is a DoS / partial-deploy vector, not a containment bypass, but it contradicts the PR's atomicity claim.",
2026-06-09T08:51:38.7117018Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/install/phases/local_content.py",

2026-06-09T08:51:41.1160240Z       "suggestion": "Widen the except clause to also catch RecursionError (and re-raise after cleanup), or add an explicit depth counter in _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated that raises PathTraversalError when depth exceeds a sane limit (e.g. 128). The except block becomes: except (PathTraversalError, RecursionError): if install_path.exists(): safe_rmtree(install_path, install_path.parent.parent); raise PathTraversalError('Package directory nesting exceeds safe depth') from exc",
2026-06-09T08:51:41.1165852Z       "evidence": {
2026-06-09T08:51:41.1166377Z         "outcome": "missing",
2026-06-09T08:51:41.1166836Z         "tier": "unit",
2026-06-09T08:51:42.3654227Z         "test_file": "tests/unit/install/test_local_content_symlink_deref.py",
2026-06-09T08:51:42.3667660Z         "test_name": "N/A -- no deep-nesting test exists",
2026-06-09T08:51:42.3701133Z         "proves": "No test verifies that a RecursionError during copy triggers the partial-install cleanup path.",
2026-06-09T08:51:43.5099291Z         "assertion_excerpt": "except PathTraversalError: ... safe_rmtree(install_path, ...) -- RecursionError not caught"
2026-06-09T08:51:43.5100865Z       }
2026-06-09T08:51:43.5101231Z     },
2026-06-09T08:51:43.5101610Z     {
2026-06-09T08:51:43.5101975Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-09T08:51:43.5131168Z       "summary": "TOCTOU window between ensure_path_within and copy2 is narrowed but not closed",
2026-06-09T08:51:46.7450773Z       "rationale": "The sequence is: (1) resolved = entry.resolve(strict=True), (2) ensure_path_within(resolved, pkg_root) validates containment, (3) shutil.copy2(resolved, dst_entry). Between steps 2 and 3 a concurrent process with write access to pkg_root could replace the file at resolved with a symlink pointing outside pkg_root. shutil.copy2 follows symlinks by default (follow_symlinks=True), so it would read the attacker's target. The PR's security.md says the guarantee is resolve -> validate -> copy2, which is accurate, but the remaining window should be documented. Exploiting this requires concurrent write access to the package source directory during install -- low practical risk for local packages the user controls, but relevant in shared or world-writable directories.",
2026-06-09T08:51:46.7457275Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/install/phases/local_content.py",
2026-06-09T08:51:46.7459877Z       "line": 169,
2026-06-09T08:51:49.1391758Z       "suggestion": "Add a note in the function docstring and in security.md that a narrow TOCTOU window remains between ensure_path_within and copy2 for the case where the package source directory is concurrently writable. If eliminating the window is required in future, use os.open with O_NOFOLLOW and os.sendfile/read via file descriptors rather than shutil.copy2(path_str). For now, documenting the residual window and the mitigation (ensure pkg source is owned by the installing user) is sufficient.",
2026-06-09T08:51:50.3118259Z       "evidence": {
2026-06-09T08:51:50.3128895Z         "outcome": "manual",
2026-06-09T08:51:50.3132008Z         "tier": "manual-only",

2026-06-09T08:51:50.3178967Z         "test_name": "N/A -- TOCTOU requires concurrent filesystem manipulation, not unit-testable",
2026-06-09T08:51:51.5164241Z         "proves": "The validate-then-copy sequence is correct under single-threaded access; the residual window is inherent to the userspace Python API.",
2026-06-09T08:51:52.8814957Z         "assertion_excerpt": "ensure_path_within(resolved, pkg_root) ... shutil.copy2(resolved, dst_entry) -- no fd-level atomicity"
2026-06-09T08:51:52.8816588Z       }
2026-06-09T08:51:52.8817010Z     },
2026-06-09T08:51:52.8817282Z     {
2026-06-09T08:51:52.8822528Z       "severity": "nit",
2026-06-09T08:51:52.8863616Z       "summary": "Partial-cleanup test asserts conditionally on install_path.exists() instead of asserting cleanup is unconditional",

TestCircularSymlink in the unit file correctly catches this, but only at unit tier. The escaping-symlink and broken-symlink integration tests would not exercise the entered instead of mutating the shared parent set. This is intentionally correct: two different symlinks pointing to the same physical directory (diamond topology) are both materialized without false circular detection, while true cycles (A->B->A) are still caught at recursion depth 3 for a 2-node cycle, safely within Python default recursion limits for any real package. symlink escapes package root]\n    N -- within root --> O{resolved.is_dir?}\n    O -- Yes and resolved in _visited --> P[raise PathTraversalError: circular symlink detected]\n    O -- Yes and not in _visited --> Q[FS recurse _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated resolved dst_entry pkg_root _visited-union-resolved]\n    Q --> R[FS shutil.copystat resolved dst_entry line 167 correct: propagates dir metadata]\n    R --> L\n    O -- No is file --> S[FS shutil.copy2 resolved dst_entry line 169]\n    S --> T[FS shutil.copystat resolved dst_entry line 170 REDUNDANT: copy2 already called copystat internally]\n    T --> L\n    L -- is_dir --> U[FS recurse _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated entry dst_entry pkg_root _visited same-ref]\n    U --> V[FS shutil.copystat entry dst_entry line 173 correct: propagates dir metadata]\n    V --> L\n    L -- is_file --> W[FS shutil.copy2 entry dst_entry line 175]\n    W --> L\n    L -- all entries done --> X[return install_path]\n    B -- PathTraversalError raised --> Y{install_path.exists?}\n    Y -- Yes --> Z[FS safe_rmtree install_path install_path.parent.parent cleanup partial]\n    Z --> AA[re-raise PathTraversalError]\n    Y -- No --> AA",
2026-06-09T08:55:28.1056412Z       "sequence": "sequenceDiagram\n    participant CLI as apm install\n    participant LCP as _copy_local_package local_content.py:178\n    participant CTDV as _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated local_content.py:90\n    participant PS as ensure_path_within path_security.py:98\n    participant FS as Filesystem\n    CLI->>LCP: dep_ref, install_path, base_dir, project_root, logger\n    LCP->>FS: local.is_dir, install_path.parent.mkdir\n    LCP->>FS: safe_rmtree install_path if exists\n    LCP->>CTDV: src=local, dst=install_path, pkg_root=local, _visited=None\n    CTDV->>FS: dst.mkdir and sorted src.iterdir\n    loop for each entry in src\n        alt entry is symlink\n            CTDV->>FS: entry.resolve strict=True\n            Note over CTDV: PathTraversalError● Read (Python-architect agent — PR 1676 architectural review)
2026-06-09T08:55:28.1061600Z   └ Running
2026-06-09T08:55:28.1061749Z 
2026-06-09T08:55:31.4039518Z  if broken target\n            CTDV->>PS: ensure_path_within resolved pkg_root\n            Note over PS: PathTraversalError if escapes pkg_root\n            alt resolved is dir and not in _visited\n                CTDV->>CTDV: recurse resolved dst_entry pkg_root _visited-union-resolved\n                CTDV->>FS: shutil.copystat resolved dst_entry line 167\n            else resolved is dir and in _visited\n                Note over CTDV: PathTraversalError circular symlink\n            else resolved is file\n                CTDV->>FS: shutil.copy2 resolved dst_entry line 169\n                CTDV->>FS: shutil.copystat resolved dst_entry line 170 REDUNDANT\n            end\n        else entry is regular directory\n            CTDV->>CTDV: recurse entry dst_entry pkg_root _visited same-ref\n            CTDV->>FS: shutil.copystat entry dst_entry line 173\n        else entry is regular file\n            CTDV->>FS: shutil.copy2 entry dst_entry line 175\n        end\n    end\n    CTDV-->>LCP: success or PathTraversalError\n    alt PathTraversalError raised\n        LCP->>FS: safe_rmtree install_path cleanup partial\n        LCP-->>CLI: raise PathTraversalError\n    else success\n        LCP-->>CLI: return install_path\n    end"

2026-06-09T09:01:24.9421563Z {
2026-06-09T09:01:27.0151922Z   "headline": "Local-path apm install now materializes in-package symlinks as real files, closing a silent data-loss bug and adding hard-fail containment guards that reach parity with remote install.",
2026-06-09T09:01:43.4061627Z   "arbitration": "The PR closes a meaningful trust gap: local-path installs previously silently dropped symlinked files while remote installs did not. All active panelists agree the core fix is architecturally sound -- escape detection via pkg_root boundary, per-branch _visited snapshot semantics for circular detection, and cleanup atomicity via safe_rmtree. The supply-chain expert and python-architect reviewed the containment logic independently and found no fundamental flaw. This convergence on correctness is the primary signal. The unit test suite covers all eight scenarios, and the two integration tests are structurally sound (pytest S7 certification pending CI run, per test-coverage-expert).\n\nThe strongest single evidence signal below the core fix is a supply-chain finding with outcome:failed: the partial-cleanup test at line 209 uses a conditional assertion (if install_path.exists(): assert not evil.exists()) rather than an unconditional assert not install_path.exists(). The conditional form silently passes if other partial content remains, directly weakening the regression trap for the PR's stated atomicity guarantee. This is a one-line pre-merge fold with no design cost. The second evidence-weighted gap is RecursionError on deep trees (outcome:missing on a security surface): a crafted package with 250-plus nested directories reaches RecursionError before the cleanup gate, leaving partial install_path unremoved. Adding a depth counter capped at 128 levels is a bounded fix. Three panelists independently flagged the CHANGELOG as incomplete for upgrading users: the escaping-symlink hard-fail is a new behavior -- CI that was previously green with an out-of-root symlink will go red on upgrade -- and an explicit migration line belongs in the changelog.\n\nDevX-UX raises a real design-debt finding: PathTraversalError is semantically overloaded for broken symlinks and permission errors that are not containment violations. The CEO decision is to defer to a follow-up issue: introducing a new exception type without coordinated catch-site updates across install.py and the test suite is a scope expansion that risks regression and should not gate this security fix. The NTFS junction bypass (supply-chain, recommended) is real but Windows-specific, requires an actively malicious local package, and is appropriately handled by a security.md note rather than blocking. The PathTraversalError handler gap in install.py (flagged convergently by cli-logging-expert and devx-ux-expert) and the install.md behavioral documentation gap are both high-priority fast-follows but do not block the security value delivered here.",
2026-06-09T09:01:50.5954400Z   "dissent_notes": "DevX-UX and cli-logging-expert diverge on error-type scope: devx-ux-expert wants a new exception class (LocalInstallError or PackageContentError) for non-containment failures; cli-logging-expert wants only a dedicated catch site for the existing PathTraversalError in install.py -- a narrower, non-breaking intervention. The CEO sides with cli-logging-expert for this PR: a dedicated catch site is bounded and achievable in a fast follow; the new exception type is a cross-cutting refactor with test-migration cost that warrants its own issue. On the partial-cleanup test assertion, supply-chain-security-expert returned outcome:failed while no other panelist noted the conditional weakness; the CEO treats this as the highest-priority pre-merge fold because it directly weakens the regression trap for the PR's own stated guarantee. 2026-06-09T09:07:49.3082866Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3084397Z   For the PR context, I'm looking at PR #1676 which fixes symlink dereferencing during local installation. The change introduces a helper function that replaces the standard `shutil.copytree` with a more careful approach that resolves each symlink, validates it doesn't escape the package boundary, and copies the dereferenced content. If a symlink tries to escape, it raises a `PathTraversalError` and fails hard.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3085924Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3305059Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3305259Z   **Hook / Story Analysis:**
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3306217Z   The core story is: "local-install parity with remote-install" - this is a real bug fix that package authors building local workflows would hit. The use case of `shared-contract.md` referenced from multiple skills via symlinks is extremely relatable.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3308683Z   The key growth signals here are that this fixes a silent data loss bug where symlinked files were being dropped without warning, which damages user trust. The behavior change to throw a PathTraversalError for escaping symlinks is a breaking change, but the right kind—it makes the tool safer. The security.md documentation with six explicit security properties is a strong trust signal for enterprise evaluators.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3310188Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3313761Z   On the adoption side, this creates parity between local and remote installs, reduces "works differently locally" complaints, and establishes security-by-default with hard failures. The test patterns with eight cases across regression, security, baseline, and edge scenarios provide a solid template for future contributions. The main friction point is that the CHANGELOG buries the impact on package authors who had escaping symlinks—it reads more like maintainer documentation than a heads-up for users who'll hit this breaking change. The threat model documentation with its six-property format is exactly what security teams want to see in compliance reviews—a strong credibility signal. But the breaking change where escaping symlinks now hard-fail instead of being silently droppe... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3752 chars]
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3316590Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3319047Z [184] ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/apm/apm && uv run pytest tests/unit/install/test_local_content_symlink_deref.py tests/unit/test_local_deps.py::TestCopyLocalPackage::test_copy_preserves_symlinks_without_following -v --tb=short 2>&1 | tail -60
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3319888Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3320228Z [185] ◐ Now I have a good picture of the PR. Let me now analyze the UX aspects from the DevX UX lens:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3320667Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3320986Z   1. **Error type semantics (`PathTraversalError` for non-security issues)**:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3322342Z      - Broken/dangling symlinks raise `PathTraversalError` - this is a type-name mismatch. A broken symlink is not a path traversal attack. Users who just have dangling symlinks in their package (perhaps a temporary state) would see `PathTraversalError` which incorrectly implies they attempted something malicious.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3323990Z      - Similarly, a `PermissionError` wrapping as `PathTraversalError` is semantically wrong.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3324684Z      - Circular symlinks get the same treatment, but they're really an infinite loop problem, not a traversal issue.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3325184Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3325409Z   2. **Recovery guidance for broken symlinks**:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3325970Z      - The error for escaping symlinks provides clear next steps: "Remove the symlink or replace it with a real file copy."
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3327034Z      - For broken symlinks, the message just states the problem without telling users how to fix it. They need to know to either remove the broken symlink or fix its target.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3328020Z      - Circular symlinks similarly lack guidance—users should be told to remove the circular symlink loop.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3328717Z      - Unreadable directories also miss the point: users need to know to check filesystem permissions.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3329199Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3329473Z   3. **`PathTraversalError` is misleading for broken symlinks**:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3330514Z      - A stale symlink like `link -> /tmp/something-that-got-deleted` triggers `PathTraversalError`, which makes it sound like a security violation when it's just a broken reference. This will confuse users who think they've done something wrong.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3331610Z      - The same issue happens when `PermissionError` gets wrapped as `PathTraversal`.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3332025Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3332216Z   4. **CHANGELOG gaps**:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3333257Z      - It correctly names the behavior change and mentions `PathTraversalError`, but doesn't tell upgrading users what to do if they have escaping symlinks or how common this problem is.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3334194Z      - It also doesn't explain the user-facing impact of broken symlinks now causing hard failures.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3334645Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3334900Z   5. **Breaking change from silent drop to hard fail**:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3335267Z      - T... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 5599 chars]
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3335498Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3349515Z

The PR adds a substantial new subsection documenting local-install symlink dereference with 26 lines of content, including 6 numbered points and threat model explanation. Nothing was cut to compensate, though this appears to be a genuine new security property worth documenting—I'd flag it as a nit rather than a bloat violation. There are also some voice and structure consistency issues emerging in the numbered items.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3356044Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3366444Z   Key findings so far:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3366666Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3368214Z   1. **PathTraversalError is a ValueError subclass** - this is important because at line 1598, the `except Exception` handler wraps the error: `logger.error(f"Error installing dependencies: {e}")` or `logger.error(f"Failed to install APM dependencies: {e}")` (line 1820-1821). Since `PathTraversalError` is NOT specifically caught (unlike `DirectDependencyError`, `PolicyViolationError`, etc.), it falls into the generic `except Exception` handler.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3370657Z   2. **PathTraversalError gets re-raised from pipeline.py** - the pipeline explicitly re-raises it at line 879 for actionable user guidance, but I need to trace where the install command actually catches or handles this exception, since the issue seems to be in how the install command processes it.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3371781Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3375184Z   Looking at the exception handlers in install.py, there are two broad catch-all blocks that wrap any Exception (including PathTraversalError) with generic error messages like "Error installing dependencies: {e}". The second handler at lines 1814-1825 does have special handling for PolicyViolationError to avoid double-nesting, but PathTraversalError isn't getting that same treatment, so it gets wrapped in the generic "Failed to install APM dependencies:" prefix instead of being surfaced directly to the user. the symlink or replace it with a real file copy. Local install aborted." - so there's redundancy where the exception message already signals failure, then gets wrapped with another failure prefix. The real issue is that `PathTraversalError` isn't explicitly caught in install.py like other exceptions are, so it falls through to the generic handler and gets the "Error installing dependencies:" prefix added, compounding the messaging problem.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3378490Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3378852Z   Also, the logger isn't being passed down to ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4395 chars]

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3397151Z [219] ◐ Now I have enough context to write my final JSON response. Let me analyze the key growth angles:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3397611Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3399056Z   1. **CHANGELOG entry** - It's clear but buries the user-benefit hook. The last sentence "Previously, in-package symlinks were silently dropped by the downstream deploy filter" is the key user-facing message - the user who hit this bug would be searching for exactly this recognition. Moving the "previously" clause to the front would be more user-facing. However, this is a `recommended` finding rather than blocking.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3400547Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3401596Z   2. **Breaking behavior for escaping symlinks** - The CHANGELOG mentions "hard-fail with PathTraversalError" for escaping symlinks, but doesn't clearly flag this as a behavior change for users who previously had these (previously they were silently dropped or followed). Now they get a hard failure. 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3402818Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3404370Z   This could break CI for some users who didn't know they had escaping symlinks. The CHANGELOG should add a callout warning that packages with symlinks pointing outside the package root will now produce a hard failure instead of being silently handled, and users should audit their packages before upgrading. The current text does mention this behavior, but it's not flagged prominently as a breaking change for users relying on the old silent-drop behavior.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3405988Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3406443Z   3. **security.md** - The threat model documentation is excellent for enterprise storytelling and evaluator conversations.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3406976Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3407510Z   4. **first-package.md** - Missing documentation on symlinks as a viable pattern for shared content, which is a documentation gap worth addressing.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3408120Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3409951Z   5. **The "silent data loss" narrative** - This is the strongest angle for growth. Silent data loss during local install erodes trust, so framing this fix as closing a trust-eroding bug rather than a technical implementation detail is more compelling. The release notes should lead with "we fixed a silent data-loss bug" and then explain that previously symlinked files were being dropped without warning. 2026-06-09T09:07:49.3426949Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3427094Z [221] ◐ Key findings so far:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3428028Z   1. NTFS junction points on Windows - `Path.is_symlink()` returns False for junction points on Windows, so junction points fall into the `elif entry.is_dir()` branch and are recursed into WITHOUT containment check. This is a Windows-specific escape.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3428965Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3429199Z   Now let me check the TOCTOU window more carefully:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3429507Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3429689Z   The sequence is:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3429987Z   1. `entry.is_symlink()` → True (at time T1)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3430446Z   2. `resolved = entry.resolve(strict=True)` → resolves target (at time T2)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3431036Z   3. `ensure_path_within(resolved, pkg_root)` → validates containment (at time T3)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3431630Z   4. Then we check if the resolved target is a directory and either copy it or recurse (at time T4-T5)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3432085Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3433750Z   The TOCTOU vulnerability window exists between the resolution check and the actual file operation—an attacker could theoretically swap the file at the resolved path with a symlink pointing outside the allowed directory, but only if they have write access to that location, which would already be a significant privilege escalation.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3435113Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3436053Z   There's also a potential race between when we call `iterdir()` to get the list of entries and when we check `is_symlink()` on each one—an attacker could create a new symlink in the package directory between those two operations.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3436911Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3438272Z   But actually, that wouldn't work because the symlink would need to already exist in the `entries` list from `iterdir()`. The real vulnerability window is after we resolve the symlink and validate it's within the package root, but before we actually copy it—an attacker could swap out the resolved file with something malicious in that gap.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3439484Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3440681Z   The catch is that this requires the attacker to have write access to the package directory while installation is happening, which is realistic in world-writable or shared directories, or if a malicious package sets up symlinks during its own install. Since APM doesn't run post-install hooks by design, this particular attack vector is limited.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3441951Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3442740Z   The real issue is the TOCTOU window between `resolve()` and `copy2()` — if someone swaps the resolved file wi... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 8096 chars]

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3459430Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3459928Z [228] ◐ Now I have a thorough understanding of the PR and its UX implications. Let me compile my findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3460435Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3460644Z   **Key UX observations:**
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3460881Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3461275Z   1. **`PathTraversalError` for non-traversal failures (broken symlinks, permission errors)**:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3461995Z      - The docstring for `PathTraversalError` says: "Raised when a computed path escapes its expected base directory."
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3463085Z      - Wrapping `PermissionError` (unreadable directory) as `PathTraversalError` is a type mismatch. A permission error is not a path traversal.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3463871Z      - Wrapping `OSError` (broken symlink) as `PathTraversalError` is a type mismatch. 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3464301Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3465976Z   A dangling symlink is just a broken reference, not a path traversal attempt. When users encounter a `PathTraversalError` for this case, they'll get a misleading message that suggests they were doing something malicious with paths, when really they just have a stale symlink left over from a rename or move operation. This is the core UX problem—the exception type doesn't match the actual error condition.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3467442Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3467947Z   Beyond the type mismatch, three of the four error cases lack any guidance on how to fix them, leaving users without clear remediation steps.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3468717Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3469677Z   The documentation also needs updating: the CLI docs mention that local paths are accepted but don't explain that symlinks within packages get dereferenced, or that symlinks escaping the package root will cause the operation to fail entirely.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3470612Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3471502Z   The CHANGELOG entry is incomplete too—it only mentions symlinks escaping the root, but doesn't call out that broken and circular symlinks now also trigger hard failures, which could surprise users during upgrades.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3472325Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3473153Z   There's also a concern with error messages leaking raw OS exception details when directory reads fail, though the absolute paths in symlink resolution errors are actually helpful for debugging.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3473923Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3475088Z   Looking at severity, the `PathTraversalError` name for broken symlinks is misleading since it's not a security issue—that's `recommended` to fix since it damages trust in the error naming. The missing remediation guidance across thr... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 5523 chars]
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3476070Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3478243Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3479130Z [231] ◐ Now I can see that `PathTraversalError` is caught in many places upstream. The error eventually gets caught and displayed. The question is whether the error message displayed to users from broken symlinks is going to confuse them.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3480043Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3481538Z   Key insight: upstream catchers in `copilot_cowork_paths.py`, `prompt_integrator.py`, `agent_integrator.py`, etc. all catch `PathTraversalError`. When the new code raises `PathTraversalError` for a broken symlink or permission error, those upstream catchers will catch it and may handle it in ways designed for security-violation cases (like logging it at a security level, or giving generic "path traversal detected" guidance). 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3483208Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3484256Z   This makes the UX problem worse. I need to trace through what happens when `_copy_local_package` raises `PathTraversalError` during the install pipeline—the exception propagates up and gets caught by whatever's orchestrating the installation process.

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3496595Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3497046Z [240] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive view of the whole picture. Let me now synthesize my findings into the required JSON output.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3497576Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3497769Z   Key findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3497974Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3498633Z   1. **PathTraversalError is NOT caught specifically in install.py** - it falls through to the generic `except Exception` handler at line 1598 or 1814, which wraps the message with:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3499509Z      - `"Error installing dependencies: <PathTraversalError message>"` (first handler)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3500148Z      - `"Failed to install APM dependencies: <PathTraversalError message>"` (second handler, for APM deps)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3500619Z      
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3500909Z      This means the user sees the error message TWICE contextualized:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3501501Z      - The exception's own suffix "Local install aborted." becomes redundant when wrapped by the generic handler
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3502202Z      - The prefix "Error installing dependencies:" or "Failed to install APM dependencies:" gets prepended
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3502807Z      
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3503637Z      The `PathTraversalError` gets special treatment in `pipeline.py` (re-raised verbatim) but NOT in `install.py`'s exception handlers, which is a gap relative to how `DirectDependencyError` and `PolicyViolationError` are handled.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3504530Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3505203Z   2. **"Local install aborted." suffix redundancy** - when rendered through the generic handler, the message becomes something like `[x] Error installing dependencies: Symlink 'X' resol...`
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3505951Z      
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3506334Z      The "Local install aborted." suffix is already implied by the error prefix, so it's just noise.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3506776Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3507640Z   3. **Logger not forwarded to `_copy_tree_dereferencing_validated`** - the function lacks a logger parameter, so users running in verbose mode get no progress feedback during the copy phase, which is inconsistent with the `--verbose` principle.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3508551Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3509292Z   4. **No verbose progress for symlink resolution** - even with `--verbose` enabled, there's nothing logged between "Installing local package..." and any error that fires, making it harder to debug large packages.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3510102Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3510596Z   5. **Circular symlink message lacks actionable guidance** - the error message identifies the problem but doesn't suggest how to fix it

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3521322Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3521894Z   1. "Each symlink is resolved per-file (resolve -> validate -> copy2) before any filesystem write for that entry." - Confirmed by code lines 96-101, 105-106
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3523064Z   2. "The resolved target is verified to remain inside the package root using the `ensure_path_within()` containment helper." - Confirmed by lines 147
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3524559Z   3. "If the resolved target escapes the package root, APM hard-fails the install with a `PathTraversalError` and a human-readable message naming the offending link. No warn-and-skip; no silent follow." - Confirmed by lines 149-153 (the message does name the offending link and includes the escape target)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3526061Z   4. "Only symlinks that resolve within the package root are dereferenced and copied as regular files. External symlinks are never followed." - Confirmed
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3527199Z   5. "Circular directory-symlink chains are detected deterministically with an explicit visited-set guard, independent of OS-level ELOOP limits." - Confirmed by lines 114-115, 122-123, 158-165
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3528745Z   6. 2026-06-09T09:07:49.3537362Z [253] ◐ Now I see the full picture. The `PathTraversalError` raised by `_copy_local_package` propagates up through `LocalDependencySource.acquire()`, then up through the install pipeline where it's caught at `pipeline.py:879` and re-raised as-is.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3538334Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3539908Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3542994Z   3. **install.md not updated**: The CLI reference doc doesn't describe the new symlink behavior for local-path installs.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3543627Z   4. **CHANGELOG missing broken/circular symlink hard-fail cases**.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3543978Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3544962Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3547151Z   The `PathTraversalError` gets raised in `_copy_tree_dereferencing_validated`, caught and re-raised by `_copy_local_package`, then propagates through `LocalDependencySource.acquire()` without being caught. By the time it reaches `pipeline.py:879`, it's surfaced as-is for the user to see. So the error message text itself is what matters for UX—the exception type is more of an internal code clarity concern, though it could still confuse maintainers and the docstring on `PathTraversalError` doesn't fully capture all the cases it's used for.

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3565009Z [263] ◐ Now I have a complete picture. Let me construct the final JSON output carefully.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3565415Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3565607Z   Summary of findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3565823Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3567910Z   1. **Recommended**: `PathTraversalError` is not caught explicitly in `install.py`. It falls through to the generic `except Exception` handler at line 1598 and 1814. The user sees the error wrapped with `"Error installing dependencies: "` or `"Failed to install APM dependencies: "` prefix. The `"Local install aborted."` suffix in the exception text is then rendered redundantly (the prefix already communicates that). `DirectDependencyError` and `PolicyViolationError` both get dedicated handlers that render `str(e)` verbatim - `PathTraversalError` deserves the same since it includes actionable fix hints.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3571097Z   2. **Recommended**: `logger` is accepted by `_copy_local_package` but never passed down to `_copy_tree_dereferencing_validated`, so users running with `--verbose` get no per-symlink tracing. The generic error handler also suggests `"Run with --verbose for detailed diagnostics"` which is misleading since verbose mode wouldn't actually add symlink-level traces.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3573237Z   3. **Nit**: Circular and broken symlink error messages lack remediation hints, while the escape-sequence error includes `"Remove the symlink or replace it with a real file copy."` — the inconsistency should be addressed.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3575395Z   4. **Nit**: The `"Local install aborted."` suffix repeats across all four exceptions, creating noise when the generic handler wraps them with `"Error installing dependencies: ..."`. Also, the cleanup in `_copy_local_package` when catching `PathTraversalError` happens silently without logging, so verbose mode provides no confirmation that the directory was actually removed. Now I'll generate the final JSON with strict ASCII encoding.

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3592066Z [265] ◐ Now I have all the context I need. Let me finalize my assessment and write the JSON.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3592609Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3592799Z   Key findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3595047Z   1. **`PathTraversalError` reused for broken symlinks and permission errors** - `recommended`: The docstring for `PathTraversalError` says "Raised when a computed path escapes its expected base directory." A broken/dangling symlink (OSError from `resolve(strict=True)`) and a `PermissionError` on `iterdir` are NOT path traversal scenarios. They'll confuse maintainers. The pipeline also specially handles `PathTraversalError` as "path-safety violations" (pipeline.py:879-882 comment: "Path-safety violation in SKILL_BUNDLE or other nested resolution"). Wrapping innocent broken symlinks in a security-named exception type pollutes this signal.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3598144Z   2. **Missing next-action guidance in 3 of 4 error messages** - `recommended`: The "failure mode is the product" principle says every error must give a concrete next step. Only the escaping-symlink case has this; the broken symlink, circular symlink, and unreadable directory cases don't.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3600016Z   3. **`install.md` not updated** - `recommended`: Per persona contract, CLI behavior changes must be in `cli-commands.md`. The install.md (which is the canonical reference for `apm install`) has no mention of the symlink dereference behavior or the new hard-fail cases for local-path installs.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3601848Z   4. **CHANGELOG entry incomplete for broken/circular symlinks** - `nit`: The CHANGELOG documents the escaping symlink hard-fail but omits that broken and circular symlinks also hard-fail now, which could surprise users upgrading with stale symlinks in their local packages.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3603202Z   5. **Formatting issue in CHANGELOG** - `nit`: Missing blank line before the `### Changed` section header.

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3623241Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3624529Z   1. "Each symlink is resolved per-file (resolve -> validate -> copy2) before any filesystem write for that entry." - ACCURATE: Code line 105-107 confirms "Each symlink undergoes a per-file resolve -> validate -> copy2 sequence rather than a bulk copytree that resolves and copies in separate passes, so the containment check fires before any filesystem write for that entry."
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3625868Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3626574Z   2. "The resolved target is verified to remain inside the package root using the `ensure_path_within()` containment helper." - ACCURATE: Code line 147 confirms `ensure_path_within(resolved, pkg_root)`.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3631783Z   3. The implementation hard-fails with a `PathTraversalError` if a symlink escapes the package root, with a message that names the offending link—no silent skipping or warnings. 4. Only symlinks resolving within the package root get dereferenced and copied as regular files; external symlinks are never followed. 5. Circular directory-symlink chains are detected deterministically using an explicit visited-set guard, independent of OS-level ELOOP limits. 6. Unreadable package directories trigger a `PathTraversalError` rather than leaking bare OS errors up the install stack. I'm also noticing the code handles two distinct cases the docs don't separate: broken or unresolvable symlink targets versus symlinks that escape the root. The full error message shows the actual path traversal case, which is helpful for documentation. I'm also noticing that while the docs reference `PathTraversalError` as the exception class name, that's actually appropriate for an enterprise security review audience rather than end users, since this page is specifically targeted at security reviewers and compliance auditors evaluating the package manager's security posture.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3636240Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3650415Z         "file": "docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/security.md",
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3650750Z         "line": 257,
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3651734Z         "suggestion": "Add a code block after item 3 showing the error format: PathTraversalError: Symlink './link' resolves to '/etc/passwd', which escapes the package root '/path/to/pkg'. Only in-package symlinks are dereference... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 5695 chars]
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3652790Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3653240Z [269] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive picture of the security properties and gaps. Let me compose the final JSON response.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3653897Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3654094Z   ## Summary of findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3654320Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3654561Z   ### 1. NTFS Junction Points on Windows (Recommended)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3654988Z   - `Path.is_symlink()` returns False for NTFS junction points on Windows
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3655514Z   - Junction points fall into the `elif entry.is_dir()` branch without containment check
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3656082Z   - A malicious package on Windows could include a junction to `C:\Windows\System32`
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3656662Z   - The traversal would copy contents of that directory to `install_path` without validation
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3657248Z   - This is a real gap, but limited to Windows, and the PR's primary context is Linux/Mac
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3657806Z   - Severity: **recommended** (not blocking - Windows is not the primary platform)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3658205Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3658536Z   ### 2. Narrow TOCTOU Window (Recommended)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3659282Z   - There's a small window between the validation check and the actual copy operation where an attacker with write access to the package directory could swap a file for a symlink
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3660260Z   - This requires the attacker to have write access during installation, which is a low practical risk for local packages
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3661322Z   - While this can't be completely eliminated without lower-level file operations using `O_NOFOLLOW`, the current approach still represents a meaningful improvement over having no validation at all
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3662282Z   - The original code's security comment acknowledged this TOCTOU issue wasn't fully resolved
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3662890Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3663127Z   ### 3. Redundant `copystat` on file symlinks (Nit)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3663721Z   - Since `shutil.copy2` already handles copying file metadata internally, the extra `copystat` call afterward is unnecessary
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3664367Z   - This is purely a code quality issue, not a security concern
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3664704Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3665019Z   ### 4. Integration test mocks bypass the security-critical path (Recommended)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3665810Z   - Most of the integration tests mock out the `_copy_local_package` function entirely, which means they're not actually exercising the security validation logic
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3666968Z   - The supply-chain-security-expert flagged this in a previous review, and there is now at least one unmocked test (`LocalInstallSymlinkDerefThroughPipeline`) that runs the full path
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3667743Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3668012Z   ### 5. Weak asserti... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4942 chars]
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3668284Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3668716Z [270] ◆ Now I have enough data to compose my complete findings. Let me do one final check on the recursion depth issue.

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3672416Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3674232Z [273] ◐ Good. Now I have all the findings. The RecursionError issue is a bit more serious than I thought - it would leave a partial install_path without cleanup because the cleanup only catches `PathTraversalError`. But in practice, a `RecursionError` on a ~245+ level nested package would be a DoS against the install command, not a security bypass (the content isn't placed anywhere dangerous). The partial install_path cleanup is the security concern.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3675963Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3676677Z   Actually, let me reconsider: a malicious package could intentionally have 300 levels of nested directories, then at level 200, have a valid file, and at level 300, trigger the RecursionError. 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3677443Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3679263Z   So when the RecursionError hits, the install_path ends up with partial content up to that point, and since the exception bypasses the cleanup handler, nothing gets removed. But on the next install attempt, the code detects the existing install_path and cleans it up before proceeding with the fresh copy. So while a partial directory does get left behind temporarily, it's not a persistent vulnerability—more of a DoS vector that gets resolved on retry.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3680867Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3682068Z   The test's assertion about atomicity is actually weaker than it claims. It only verifies that the attacker's symlink target doesn't exist if the directory is still there, but doesn't confirm the entire directory was actually cleaned up. That's a gap in the test coverage, though looking at the actual cleanup logic...

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3723945Z [291] ◐ This is a critical finding! `shutil.copy2` already calls `shutil.copystat` internally. In the PR, after `shutil.copy2(resolved, dst_entry)`, there's an additional `shutil.copystat(resolved, dst_entry)` call. This is a redundant double-stat call but it's not incorrect – it just sets the same metadata twice. Let me verify this.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3725151Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3725345Z   Looking at line 169-170:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3725589Z   ```python
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3725804Z   shutil.copy2(resolved, dst_entry)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3726099Z   shutil.copystat(resolved, dst_entry)
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3726365Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3726559Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3726998Z   `shutil.copy2` calls `shutil.copystat` internally, so the second `shutil.copystat` is redundant. This is a nit.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3730128Z   Both calls target the same `resolved` path, confirming the redundancy. For directories though, the pattern is different – `copystat` is called after the recursive copy to preserve directory metadata, which makes sense. less, just inefficient. Let me trace through this more carefully—`shutil.copy2` copies both the file data and its metadata using `copystat` internally, so calling `copystat` again on the same file afterward is definitely redundant. The directory case is different though: after recursively copying the tree, we need that final `copystat` call to preserve the directory's own metadata since the recursive function doesn't handle that on the parent level.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3732590Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3733465Z   Now I'm checking the `_visited` parameter handling—when it's a regular directory, the set gets passed through by reference, but there's something odd about how it's being initialized in the recursive calls.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3734297Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3736757Z   For symlink-to-directory cases, the code creates a new set with `_visited | {resolved}` rather than mutating the original, which means the parent's `_visited` never gets updated with entries from the symlink recursion. This could be a real problem if there are circular references or overlapping symlinks, since the tracking isn't consistent across the recursion tree. empty), so it's not flagged as circular. This is the correct behavior—multiple symlinks pointing to the same directory from different locations should be allowed and copied separately. But in a tru... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 19630 chars]
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3738850Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3741319Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3742105Z [294] ◐ OK, `robust_rmtree` uses `shutil.rmtree` which removes the entire directory tree. So the cleanup is correct for the `PathTraversalError` case. The concern is that `RecursionError` bypasses the cleanup.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3743042Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3743311Z   Now let me finalize my JSON response. Here are my findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3743649Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3744655Z   1. **Windows NTFS Junction Bypass** (recommended): `Path.is_symlink()` returns False for NTFS junctions on Windows. They fall into `elif entry.is_dir()` without containment check. A malicious local package could use a junction to C:\Windows\System32 to exfiltrate content into install_path.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3746688Z   2. **TOCTOU Window Not Fully Closed** (recommended): Between the path validation and the actual copy operation, a concurrent process could swap the file with a symlink pointing outside the allowed directory. The security documentation mentions validating before writing, but doesn't account for this race condition window.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3748534Z   3. **RecursionError Bypasses Cleanup Gate** (recommended): The recursive tree-copying function has no recursion depth limit, and when a `RecursionError` occurs, it escapes the error handler that would normally trigger cleanup.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3750070Z   4. **Weak Partial-Cleanup Test Assertion** (nit): The test for atomicity should unconditionally verify the install path doesn't exist, rather than conditionally checking for a specific file.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3751194Z   5. **Redundant copystat for file symlinks** (nit): The `copy2` operation already copies file metadata, so the subsequent `copystat` call is redundant.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3751816Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3752026Z   Now I'll compile the final JSON output.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3752229Z

The code's is_symlink() guard returns False, so the entry falls into the elif entry.is_dir() branch and is recursed into via _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated(entry, dst_entry, pkg_root, _visited) with no containment check -- the pkg_root boundary is never applied. 2026-06-09T09:07:49.3767428Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3767693Z [296] ◐ Now I have all the information I need. Let me confirm my findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3768063Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3768356Z   1. `shutil.copy2` DOES call `shutil.copystat` internally (confirmed).
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3769975Z   2. On Windows with `_winapi.CopyFile2`, there's a fast path that copies metadata as part of the CopyFile2 call itself, AND there's no separate `copystat` call at the end... wait, actually looking at the code: on Windows with `_winapi.CopyFile2`, if the copy succeeds, it returns immediately WITHOUT calling `copystat`. But `CopyFile2` on Windows DOES copy metadata. So the behavior is different on Windows vs non-Windows.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3772254Z   3. Across all code paths—whether non-Windows, Windows fast path, or Windows fallback—metadata always gets copied, either through `copystat` or as part of the `CopyFile2` operation itself.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3773147Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3773678Z   4. This means the extra `shutil.copystat` call on line 170 is redundant; it's applying metadata a second time, which is harmless but wasteful.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3774273Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3774946Z   Now I'm looking at `robust_copy2` to see how it handles copying—it attempts a reflink clone first, then falls back to `shutil.copy2` if that doesn't work.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3775951Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3776287Z [297] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/ut..., view_range: [200, 310])
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3776627Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3777462Z [298] ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/apm/apm && git --no-pager log --oneline -5 && git --no-pager diff origin/main...HEAD -- src/apm_cli/install/phases/local_content.py 2>/dev/null | head -120
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3778120Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3779051Z [299] ◐ Now I see that at line 143: `ctx.dep_base_dirs = {}`. So `_make_ctx` sets `ctx.dep_base_dirs = {}`. This means `getattr(ctx, "dep_base_dirs", {}).get(dep_key)` will return `None` (since `{}` is an empty dict), so `base_dir = ctx.source_root = tmp_path`. This is correct!
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3779985Z

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3856053Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3856254Z   No blocking findings.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3858287Z   Generating JSON output... The risk is that a future maintainer might misinterpret the per-branch snapshot semantics and mutate _visited directly, breaking diamond topology handling. I should add a docstring note to _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated explaining that _visited uses immutable snapshots—creating a new set object per symlinked-dir entry rather than mutating in place—so legitimate packages with multiple symlinks to the same directory aren't incorrectly rejected.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3860038Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3861126Z   I'm also noticing that shutil.copystat on line 170 is redundant after shutil.copy2 for the symlink-to-file branch, since copy2 already handles metadata copying internally. The copystat calls for directories (lines 167 and 173) are necessary though, as they propagate metadata to newly created directories.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3862270Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3863229Z   There's also a missing type annotation on the _visited parameter—it should be set[Path] | None to match the codebase standard where src, dst, and pkg_root all have Path type hints.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3863959Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3865967Z   I'm also noticing that the code uses raw shutil.copy2 at lines 169 and 175, but the established pattern in github_downloader.py and artifactory_orchestrator.py is to use robust_copy2 from apm_cli.utils.file_ops instead, which attempts a reflink clone first for better performance on the same filesystem before falling back to shutil.copy2. 2026-06-09T09:07:49.3871298Z     "findings": [
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3871506Z       {
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3871710Z         "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3872228Z         "summary": "_visited per-branch snapshot semantics are correct but silently breakable by a future maintainer",
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3877011Z         "rationale": "Design patterns -- Used in this PR: Guard-then-recurse -- _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated implements a recursive descent with a per-entry validate-before-write guard (annotated in class diagram). The _visited set uses per-branch immutable snapshot semantics: _visited | {resolved} creates a new set object each time a symlinked directory is entered instead of mutating the shared parent set. This is intentionally correct: two different symlinks pointing to the same physical directory (diamond topology) are both materialized without false circular detection, while true cycles (A->B->A) are still caught at recursion depth 3 for a 2-node cycle, safely within Python default recursion limits for any real package. The risk: a future maintainer seeing _visited threaded through recursive calls may assume it is a globally-accumulated accumulator and replace _visited | {resolved} with _visited.add(resolved), which would break diamond sharing and incorrectly abort legitimate packages that have two skills symlinking into the same shared directory. A single docstring sentence is the minimal defensive fix. Pragmatic suggestion: none -- the current shape is the simplest correct design at this scope.",

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3909190Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3909895Z   1. **PathTraversalError handler in install.py** (cli-logging, devx-ux both flag this) - double-wrapping, misleading verbose trace. This is a UX debt that should be addressed but isn't a blocker.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3911151Z   2. **CHANGELOG clarity** (oss-growth, devx-ux, doc-writer) - the user-impact lead is buried and the hard-fail behavior change isn't clearly called out, plus there's a missing blank line.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3912152Z   3. **install.md not updated** (devx-ux, doc-writer) - the behavioral change needs to be reflected in the reference docs.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3913217Z   4. **Error type design** (devx-ux) - PathTraversalError is being misused for non-containment failures, which is design debt.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3913974Z   5. **NTFS junction bypass** (supply-chain) - Windows-specific concern, recommended but not blocking.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3914591Z   6. **RecursionError partial-cleanup** (supply-chain) - still reviewing this one.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3915530Z   7. **Test-coverage gaps** (test-coverage-expert) - the integration tests are structurally sound but S7-unverifiable, and there are two missing integration tests for broken and circular symlinks.
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3916306Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3918327Z   Looking at the evidence, the RecursionError finding has a missing test that would verify atomicity during cleanup—that's a security surface issue. The partial-cleanup test has a failed outcome because the conditional assertion weakens the regression trap, which is the strongest signal that the atomicity guarantee isn't properly asserted. The test-coverage findings show unknown outcomes for S7-unverifiable integration tests and missing coverage elsewhere.

2026-06-09T09:07:49.3938508Z     "headline": "Local-path apm install now materializes in-package symlinks as real files, closing a silent data-loss bug and adding hard-fail containment guards that reach parity with remote install.",
2026-06-09T09:07:49.3944776Z     "arbitration": "The PR closes a meaningful trust gap: local-path installs previously silently dropped symlinked files while remote installs did not. All active panelists agree the core fix is architecturally sound -- escape detection via pkg_root boundary, per-branch _visited snapshot semantics for circular detection, and cleanup atomicity via safe_rmtree. The supply-chain expert and python-architect reviewed the containment logic independently and found no fundamental flaw. This convergence on correctness is the primary signal. The unit test suite covers all eight scenarios, and the two integration tests are structurally sound (pytest S7 certification pending CI run, per test-coverage-expert).\n\nThe strongest single evidence signal below the core fix is a supply-chain finding with outcome:failed: the partial-cleanup test at line 209 uses a conditional assertion (if install_path.exists(): assert not evil.exists()) rather than an unconditional assert not install_path.exists(). The conditional form silently passes if other partial content remains, directly weakening the regression trap for the PR's stated atomicity guarantee. This is a one-line pre-merge fold with no design cost. The second evidence-weighted gap is RecursionError on deep trees (outcome:missing on a security surface): a crafted package with 250-plus nested directories reaches RecursionError before the cleanup gate, leaving partial install_path unremoved. Adding a depth counter capped at 128 levels is a bounded fix. Three panelists independently flagged the CHANGELOG as incomplete for upgrading users: the escaping-symlink hard-fail is a new behavior -- CI that was previously green with an out-of-root symlink will go red on upgrade -- and an explicit migration line belongs in the changelog.\n\... Adds a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 2 comment(s) can be added. Supports reply_to_id for discussion threading.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"body":{"description":"The comment text in Markdown format. Must be the final intended comment — not a placeholder or test value. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation.","type":"string"},"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"item_number":{"description":"The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 123 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/123). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.","type":["number","string"]},"pr":{"description":"Alias for item_number when targeting a pull request. Prefer item_number in new calls.","type":["number","string"]},"reply_to_id":{"description":"Node ID of the discussion comment to reply to, enabling threaded discussion comments. When provided, the new comment is posted as a reply to the specified top-level discussion comment. If the given node ID belongs to a nested reply, the handler automatically resolves it to the top-level parent. Only applicable for discussion comments — ignored for issue and pull request comments.","type":"string"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"},"temporary_id":{"description":"Unique temporary identifier for this comment. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Auto-generated if not provided. The temporary ID is returned in the tool response so you can reference this comment later.","pattern":"^aw_[A-Za-z0-9]{3,12}$","type":"string"}},"required":["body"],"type":"object"},"name":"add_comment"},{"description":"Remove labels from an existing GitHub issue or pull request. If a label is not present on the item, it will be silently skipped. Use this to clean up labels that are no longer applicable. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 3 label(s) can be removed. Only these labels can be removed: [panel-review panel-approved panel-rejected].","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"item_number":{"description":"Issue or PR number to remove labels from. If omitted, removes labels from the item that triggered this workflow.","type":"number"},"labels":{"description":"Label names to remove (e.g., ['bug', 'needs-triage']). Labels that don't exist on the item are silently skipped. At least one label must be provided.","items":{"type":"string"},"minItems":1,"type":"array"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"}},"required":["labels"],"type":"object"},"name":"remove_labels"},{"description":"Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. 2026-06-09T09:07:49.6055972Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-09T08:42:43.443Z","event":"rpc_request","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"OUT","server_id":"github","method":"tools/call","payload":{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"arguments":{"method":"get","owner":"microsoft","pullNumber":"1676","repo":"apm"},"name":"pull_request_read"}}}
2026-06-09T09:07:49.6070991Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-09T08:42:44.269Z","event":"rpc_response","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"IN","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":1676,\"title\":\"fix(install): dereference in-package symlinks on local install (#1668)\",\"body\":\"fix(install): dereference in-package symlinks on local install\\n\\n## TL;DR\\n\\nLocal-path `apm install` now materializes in-package symlinks as real files,\\nmatching the behavior of remote install. A symlink whose resolved target escapes\\nthe package root causes a hard-fail (`PathTraversalError`), never a silent drop.\\nAll five mandatory security guardrails from the maintainer brief are implemented.\\n\\nCloses #1668\\n\\n## Problem (WHY)\\n\\n\\u0026gt; \\u0026#34;A local-path `apm install ` silently drops skill files that are\\n\\u0026gt; symlinks; a remote `apm install owner/repo` of the same package keeps them.\\u0026#34;\\n\\u0026gt; -- Issue #1668\\n\\nThe divergence had two root causes:\\n\\n- `_copy_local_package` called `shutil.copytree(..., symlinks=True)`, preserving\\n  every symlink in `apm_modules/` rather than materializing its content.\\n- The downstream deploy filter `ignore_non_content` (security/gate.py) excluded\\n  *all* symlinks from the copy into target directories -- including in-package\\n  ones that were safe to follow.\\n- Result: both operations reported success while the referenced files vanished.\\n\\nThe old code also carried an explicit `SECURITY` comment acknowledging an open\\nTOCTOU window and deferring the fix -- this PR closes that window.\\n\\n## Approach (WHAT)\\n\\n| Layer | Change |\\n|-------|--------|\\n| `local_content.py` | Replace bulk `copytree(symlinks=True)` with `_copy_tree_dereferencing_validated()` |\\n| New helper | Atomic per-symlink: `resolve(strict=True)` -\\u0026gt; `ensure_path_within()` -\\u0026gt; `copy2` |\\n| Escaping symlinks | Raise `PathTraversalError` immediately; no warn-and-skip, no partial copy |\\n| `ignore_non_content` (gate.py) | Unchanged -- symlinks no longer reach deployment because they are dereferenced at staging |\\n| Security docs | Threat-model note added to `docs/enterprise/security.md` |\\n\\n## Implementation (HOW)\\n\\n**`src/apm_cli/install/phases/local_content.py`**\\n\\nAdded `_copy_tree_dereferencing_validated(src, dst, pkg_root)`: walks the source\\ntree recursively. For each symlink entry it atomically calls\\n`entry.resolve(strict=True)`, passes the result to `ensure_path_within(resolved,\\npkg_root)` (the #596 containment helper), then `shutil.copy2(resolved, dst_entry)`\\nfor files or recurses for directories. Any symlink escaping `pkg_root` raises\\n`PathTraversalError` with a human-readable message and aborts the entire copy.\\nReplaced the `shutil.copytree(symlinks=True)` call and the accompanying\\nunresolved TOCTOU note with a single call to the new helper.\\n\\n**`tests/unit/install/test_local_content_symlink_deref.py`** (new)\\n\\nSix typed-coverage tests written as a failing regression trap *before* the fix:\\n- `TestInPackageSymlinkIsDerefed` -- in-package symlink materialized as real file\\n- `TestEscapingSymlinkHardFails` -- escaping symlink raises `PathTraversalError`; no partial copy\\n- `TestNonSymlinkFilesUnchanged` -- non-symlink baseline unchanged\\n\\n**`tests/unit/test_local_deps.py`**\\n\\nUpdated `test_copy_preserves_symlinks_without_following` to assert the new\\ncorrect behavior (hard-fail) instead of the old incorrect behavior\\n(preserved-as-symlink with silent downstream drop).\\n\\n**`docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/security.md`**\\n\\nAdded \\u0026#34;Local-install symlink dereference and containment guarantee\\u0026#34; subsection\\nunder Symlink handling, documenting all four security properties.\\n\\n## Diagrams\\n\\n```mermaid\\nflowchart TD\\n    A[_copy_local_package called] --\\u0026gt; B[_copy_tree_dereferencing_validated]\\n    B --\\u0026gt; C{entry type?}\\n    C -- regular file --\\u0026gt; D[shutil.copy2 to dst]\\n    C -- directory --\\u0026gt; E[recurse]\\n    C -- symlink --\\u0026gt; F[resolve strict=True]\\n    F --\\u0026gt; G{resolved within pkg_root?}\\n    G -- yes --\\u0026gt; H[copy2 resolved content to dst]\\n    G -- no --\\u0026gt; I[raise PathTraversalError\\u003cbr/\\u003einstall aborted]\\n    D --\\u0026gt; J[continue to next entry]\\n    H --\\u0026gt; J\\n    E --\\u0026gt; J\\n```\\n\\n```mermaid\\nsequenceDiagram\\n    participant Caller as _copy_local_package\\n    participant Helper as _copy_tree_dereferencing_validated\\n    participant PW as ensure_path_within\\n    participant FS as shutil.copy2\\n\\n    Call
